ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese spicy rice salad; 'rice dish' (FOODON_00002473) is the closest broader class. Exact term unavailable.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (rice-based composite dish) used as the closest defensible broader category. No specific code exists for Burmese htamin hnay in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of spicy rice salad as served at a Singapore hawker stall is approximately 250 g, comprising a mound of seasoned rice tossed with chilli, fish sauce, shrimp paste, and topped with a fried egg.

nutrient_source.title: Spicy Rice Salad (ဇလုံ ထမင်းနယ် ကြက်ဥကြော်ပါမယ်, No Meat) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010610). All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 7.5 g/100g based on the high total fat (44.54 g/100g) and the known composition of cooking oil and shrimp paste used in this dish class; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese spicy rice salad. GI estimated at 64 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: white rice mixed dish with condiments, per University of Sydney GI Database (white rice GI ~64–72). The very low carbohydrate content (5.9 g/100g) and high fat content (44.54 g/100g) would suppress the effective glycaemic response significantly. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (64 × 5.9 / 100) × 2.5 = 9.44, rounded to 9.4 — classified as low GL despite medium GI due to low carbohydrate density.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; driven by fish sauce and shrimp paste with negligible potassium offset (64 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, fish sauce and shrimp paste should be substantially reduced and lime juice and fresh herbs used as primary seasoning to bring sodium below 400 mg/100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high fat content (44.54 g/100g) and caloric density (430 kcal/100g) with zero dietary fibre impair insulin sensitivity and promote adiposity, creating a metabolically unfavourable profile for Type 2 Diabetes management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish in its current form; oil should be reduced by at least 75%, fibre-rich vegetables (e.g., cucumber, bean sprouts) should be added, and portion size should be limited to no more than 100 g if consumed occasionally.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 44.54 g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~7.5 g/100g, sodium 810 mg/100g, and cholesterol 26 mg/100g collectively elevate LDL-C, blood pressure, and cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to its combined high saturated fat, high sodium, and high caloric density; if modified, oil must be reduced substantially and sodium-contributing condiments replaced with low-sodium alternatives.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g exceeds RA threshold; estimated saturated fat ~7.5 g/100g exceeds the 5 g gate; chilli (nightshade) may exacerbate joint inflammation; zero dietary fibre provides no gut microbiome benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; nightshade ingredient (chilli) should be monitored for joint flare, fish sauce and oil reduced substantially, and anti-inflammatory spices such as turmeric or ginger considered as substitutes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Rice Salad (No Meat), known in Burmese as ဇလုံ ထမင်းနယ် (Za Lon Htamin Hnay), is a Burmese-origin rice salad dish popular in Singapore hawker centres. It consists of cooked white rice tossed vigorously with a pungent, spicy dressing of fish sauce, shrimp paste (ngapi), chilli, lime juice, dried shrimp, garlic, and onion, then topped with a fried egg. The dish has a bold, intensely savoury and spicy flavour profile with a soft, slightly sticky rice texture and a crispy fried egg topping. The no-meat variant omits chicken or pork but retains seafood-derived condiments (fish sauce, shrimp paste, dried shrimp), making it unsuitable for vegetarians or vegans. Nutritionally, this dish is extremely high in fat (44.54 g/100g) and calories (430 kcal/100g) due to the generous use of cooking oil, with very low carbohydrate content (5.9 g/100g) and negligible dietary fibre (0 g/100g). Sodium is very high at 810 mg/100g, driven by fish sauce and shrimp paste. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. It is typically served as a lunch or dinner dish in Singapore, particularly in hawker stalls catering to the Burmese migrant community.
common_user_queries: What is spicy rice salad no meat?
Is spicy rice salad suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010610) with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input. The HPB is a primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from total fat composition and is the only derived value.
notes: Saturated fat (7.5 g/100g) is estimated from total fat (44.54 g/100g) based on the known fatty acid profile of cooking oil and shrimp paste used in this dish class; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this Burmese hawker rice salad preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source entry. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice mixed dish (GI ~64) from the University of Sydney GI Database; actual GI measurement for this specific preparation is not available. The dish contains allergens: egg, fish, crustaceans/shellfish. Not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to fish sauce, shrimp paste, and dried shrimp. Halal status uncertain due to shrimp paste preparation methods.
